findings.description: Session tokens are generated with the non-cryptographic random module (Mersenne Twister), making them predictable and enabling session hijacking; use the secrets module instead.
touch() reads _sessions directly without purging or checking expiry, so an expired-but-not-yet-purged token can be resurrected and renewed indefinitely.
user_sessions() does not purge expired sessions first (unlike get/active_count), so it returns stale expired sessions, an inconsistent contract.
The shared _sessions dict is mutated by create/touch/destroy/purge with no lock, so concurrent access in a multi-threaded server risks races and dictionary-changed-size-during-iteration errors.
_purge_expired runs on every get() and scans all sessions, making each lookup O(n) and degrading under high session counts.
create() does not check for token collision and silently overwrites any existing session sharing the generated token.
Expiry is based on time.time() wall clock, which can jump backward on NTP adjustment and cause premature expiry or extended session lifetime; time.monotonic would be more robust.
__init__ does not validate ttl, so a non-positive ttl creates sessions that are already expired on creation.
The module-level logger is defined but never used anywhere, making it dead code.
